content:
# Genesis Chapter 46 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 27r–27v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_046_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_046_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_046_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_046_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`
**View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0050 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0051

---

*Jacob Journeys to Beersheba — Sacrifices to the God of His Father Isaac (vv. 1–4)*

**46:1** And Israel set out [וַיִּסַּע יִשְׂרָאֵל] and all that was his [וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ] — and he came to Beersheba [וַיָּבֹא בְּאֵרָה שָׁבַע] and he sacrificed [וַיִּזְבַּח] sacrifices [זְבָחִים] to the God of his father Isaac [לֵאלֹהֵי אָבִיו יִצְחָק].

**46:2** And God spoke [וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים] to Israel [לְיִשְׂרָאֵל] in the visions of the night [בְּמַרְאֹת הַלָּיְלָה] and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Jacob! Jacob! [יַעֲקֹב יַעֲקֹב] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Here I am [הִנֵּנִי].

**46:3** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I am God [אָנֹכִי הָאֵל] — the God of your father [אֱלֹהֵי אָבִיךָ] — do not fear [אַל-תִּירָא] from going down to Egypt [מֵרְדָה מִצְרַיְמָה] — for into a great nation [כִּי-לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל] I will make you there [אֲשִׂימְךָ שָׁם].

**46:4** I myself will go down with you [אָנֹכִי אֵרֵד עִמְּךָ] to Egypt [מִצְרַיְמָה] — and I will also surely bring you up [וְאָנֹכִי אַעַלְךָ גַם-עָלֹה] — and Joseph will put his hand [וְיוֹסֵף יָשִׁית יָדוֹ] on your eyes [עַל-עֵינֶיךָ].

---

*Jacob and All His Household Depart for Egypt (vv. 5–7)*

**46:5** And Jacob arose [וַיָּקָם יַעֲקֹב] from Beersheba [מִבְּאֵר שָׁבַע] — and the sons of Israel [וַיִּשְׂאוּ בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל] carried Jacob their father [אֶת-יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם] and their little ones [וְאֶת-טַפָּם] and their wives [וְאֶת-נְשֵׁיהֶם] on the wagons [בָּעֲגָלוֹת] that Pharaoh had sent [אֲשֶׁר-שָׁלַח פַּרְעֹה] to carry him [לָשֵׂאת אֹתוֹ].

**46:6** And they took [וַיִּקְחוּ] their livestock [אֶת-מִקְנֵיהֶם] and their property [וְאֶת-רְכוּשָׁם] that they had acquired [אֲשֶׁר רָכְשׁוּ] in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] — and they came to Egypt [וַיָּבֹאוּ מִצְרָיְמָה] — Jacob [יַעֲקֹב] and all his seed with him [וְכָל-זַרְעוֹ אִתּוֹ].

**46:7** His sons and the sons of his sons with him [בָּנָיו וּבְנֵי-בָנָיו אִתּוֹ] — his daughters and the daughters of his sons [בְּנֹתָיו וּבְנוֹת-בָּנָיו] — and all his seed [וְכָל-זַרְעוֹ] he brought with him to Egypt [הֵבִיא אִתּוֹ מִצְרָיְמָה].

---

*The Register of All Israel Who Came to Egypt — Seventy Souls (vv. 8–27)*

**46:8** And these are the names [וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת] of the sons of Israel [בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל] who came to Egypt [הַבָּאִים מִצְרָיְמָה] — Jacob and his sons [יַעֲקֹב וּבָנָיו]: the firstborn of Jacob [בְּכֹר יַעֲקֹב] — Reuben [רְאוּבֵן].

**46:9** And the sons of Reuben [וּבְנֵי רְאוּבֵן]: Hanoch [חֲנוֹךְ] and Pallu [וּפַלּוּא] and Hezron [וְחֶצְרֹן] and Carmi [וְכַרְמִי].

**46:10** And the sons of Simeon [וּבְנֵי שִׁמְעוֹן]: Jemuel [יְמוּאֵל] and Jamin [וְיָמִין] and Ohad [וְאֹהַד] and Jachin [וְיָכִין] and Zohar [וְצֹחַר] and Shaul the son of the Canaanite woman [וְשָׁאוּל בֶּן-הַכְּנַעֲנִית].

**46:11** And the sons of Levi [וּבְנֵי לֵוִי]: Gershon [גֵּרְשׁוֹן] and Kohath [וּקְהָת] and Merari [וּמְרָרִי].

**46:12** And the sons of Judah [וּבְנֵי יְהוּדָה]: Er [עֵר] and Onan [וְאוֹנָן] and Shelah [וְשֵׁלָה] and Perez [וָפֶרֶץ] and Zerah [וָזֶרַח] — and Er died [וַיָּמָת עֵר] and Onan died [וְאוֹנָן] in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] — and the sons of Perez were [וַיִּהְיוּ בְנֵי-פֶרֶץ]: Hezron [חֶצְרוֹן] and Hamul [וְחָמוּל].

**46:13** And the sons of Issachar [וּבְנֵי יִשָּׂשכָר]: Tola [תּוֹלָע] and Puvah [וּפוּוָה] and Job [וְיוֹב] and Shimron [וְשִׁמְרֹן].

**46:14** And the sons of Zebulun [וּבְנֵי זְבוּלֻן]: Sered [סֶרֶד] and Elon [וְאֵלוֹן] and Jahleel [וְיַחְלְאֵל].

**46:15** These are the sons of Leah [אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי לֵאָה] whom she bore to Jacob [אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה לְיַעֲקֹב] in Paddan-aram [בְּפַדַּן אֲרָם] — and also Dinah his daughter [וְאֵת דִּינָה בִתּוֹ] — all the souls [כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ] of his sons and his daughters [בָּנָיו וּבְנֹתָיו]: thirty-three [שְׁלֹשִׁים וְשָׁלֹשׁ].

**46:16** And the sons of Gad [וּבְנֵי גָד]: Ziphion [צִפְיוֹן] and Haggi [וְחַגִּי] — Shuni [שׁוּנִי] and Ezbon [וְאֶצְבֹּן] — Eri [עֵרִי] and Arodi [וַאֲרוֹדִי] and Areli [וְאַרְאֵלִי].

**46:17** And the sons of Asher [וּבְנֵי אָשֵׁר]: Imnah [יִמְנָה] and Ishvah [וְיִשְׁוָה] and Ishvi [וְיִשְׁוִי] and Beriah [וּבְרִיעָה] — and Serah their sister [וְשֶׂרַח אֲחֹתָם] — and the sons of Beriah [וּבְנֵי בְרִיעָה]: Heber [חֶבֶר] and Malchiel [וּמַלְכִּיאֵל].

**46:18** These are the sons of Zilpah [אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי זִלְפָּה] — whom Laban gave [אֲשֶׁר-נָתַן לָבָן] to Leah his daughter [לְלֵאָה בִתּוֹ] — and she bore these [וַתֵּלֶד אֶת-אֵלֶּה] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב]: sixteen souls [שֵׁשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה נָפֶשׁ].

**46:19** The sons of Rachel the wife of Jacob [בְּנֵי רָחֵל אֵשֶׁת יַעֲקֹב]: Joseph [יוֹסֵף] and Benjamin [וּבִנְיָמִן].

**46:20** And to Joseph in the land of Egypt [וַיִּוָּלֵד לְיוֹסֵף בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] were born [אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה-לּוֹ] — whom Asenath daughter of Poti-pherah priest of On bore to him [אָסְנַת בַּת-פּוֹטִי פֶרַע כֹּהֵן אֹן] — Manasseh [מְנַשֶּׁה] and Ephraim [וְאֶפְרָיִם].

**46:21** And the sons of Benjamin [וּבְנֵי בִנְיָמִן]: Bela [בֶּלַע] and Becher [וּבֶכֶר] and Ashbel [וְאַשְׁבֵּל] — Gera [גֵּרָא] and Naaman [וְנַעֲמָן] — Ehi [אֵחִי] and Rosh [וָרֹאשׁ] — Muppim [מֻפִּים] and Huppim [וְחֻפִּים] and Ard [וָאָרְדְּ].

**46:22** These are the sons of Rachel [אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי רָחֵל] who were born to Jacob [אֲשֶׁר יֻלַּד לְיַעֲקֹב] — all the souls: fourteen [כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ אַרְבָּעָה עָשָׂר].

**46:23** And the sons of Dan [וּבְנֵי דָן]: Hushim [חֻשִׁים].

**46:24** And the sons of Naphtali [וּבְנֵי נַפְתָּלִי]: Jahzeel [יַחְצְאֵל] and Guni [וְגוּנִי] and Jezer [וְיֵצֶר] and Shillem [וְשִׁלֵּם].

**46:25** These are the sons of Bilhah [אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי בִלְהָה] — whom Laban gave [אֲשֶׁר-נָתַן לָבָן] to Rachel his daughter [לְרָחֵל בִּתּוֹ] — and she bore these [וַתֵּלֶד אֶת-אֵלֶּה] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב]: all the souls: seven [כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ שִׁבְעָה].

**46:26** All the souls who came with Jacob to Egypt [כָּל-הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַבָּאָה לְיַעֲקֹב מִצְרַיְמָה] — those who came out of his loins [יֹצְאֵי יְרֵכוֹ] — apart from the wives of Jacob's sons [מִלְּבַד נְשֵׁי בְנֵי-יַעֲקֹב]: all the souls were sixty-six [כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ שִׁשִּׁים וָשֵׁשׁ].

**46:27** And the sons of Joseph who were born to him in Egypt: two souls [בְּנֵי יוֹסֵף אֲשֶׁר-יֻלַּד-לוֹ בְמִצְרַיִם נֶפֶשׁ שְׁנָיִם] — all the souls of the house of Jacob [כָּל-הַנֶּפֶשׁ לְבֵית-יַעֲקֹב] who came to Egypt [הַבָּאָה מִצְרַיְמָה]: seventy [שִׁבְעִים].

---

*Judah Sent Ahead — Joseph Comes to Goshen (vv. 28–30)*

**46:28** And he sent Judah before him [וְאֶת-יְהוּדָה שָׁלַח לְפָנָיו] to Joseph [אֶל-יוֹסֵף] to direct before him [לְהוֹרֹת לְפָנָיו] to Goshen [גֹּשְׁנָה] — and they came [וַיָּבֹאוּ] to the land of Goshen [אַרְצָה גֹּשֶׁן].

**46:29** And Joseph harnessed his chariot [וַיֶּאְסֹר יוֹסֵף מֶרְכַּבְתּוֹ] and went up [וַיַּעַל] to meet Israel his father [לִקְרַאת-יִשְׂרָאֵל אָבִיו] to Goshen [גֹּשְׁנָה] — and he appeared before him [וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו] and fell upon his neck [וַיִּפֹּל עַל-צַוָּארָיו] and wept upon his neck again and again [וַיֵּבְךְּ עַל-צַוָּארָיו עוֹד].

**46:30** And Israel said to Joseph [וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל-יוֹסֵף]: Now let me die [אָמוּתָה הַפָּעַם] — since I have seen your face [אַחֲרֵי רְאוֹתִי אֶת-פָּנֶיךָ] — that you are still alive [כִּי עוֹדְךָ חָי].

---

*Joseph Instructs His Brothers — Shepherds, an Abomination to Egypt (vv. 31–34)*

**46:31** And Joseph said to his brothers [וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-אֶחָיו] and to his father's household [וְאֶל-בֵּית אָבִיו]: I will go up [אֶעֱלֶה] and tell Pharaoh [וְאַגִּידָה לְפַרְעֹה] and say to him [וְאֹמְרָה אֵלָיו]: My brothers [אַחַי] and my father's household [וּבֵית-אָבִי] who were in the land of Canaan [אֲשֶׁר בְּאֶרֶץ-כְּנָעַן] have come to me [בָּאוּ אֵלָי].

**46:32** And the men are shepherds of flocks [וְהָאֲנָשִׁים רֹעֵי צֹאן] — for men of livestock they have been [כִּי-אַנְשֵׁי מִקְנֶה הָיוּ] — and their flocks [וְצֹאנָם] and their herds [וּבְקָרָם] and all that is theirs they have brought [וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לָהֶם הֵבִיאוּ].

**46:33** And it shall be when Pharaoh calls you [וְהָיָה כִּי-יִקְרָא לָכֶם פַּרְעֹה] and says [וְאָמַר]: What is your occupation [מַה-מַּעֲשֵׂיכֶם]?

**46:34** Then you shall say [וַאֲמַרְתֶּם]: Men of livestock have been your servants [אַנְשֵׁי מִקְנֶה הָיוּ עֲבָדֶיךָ] from our youth and until now [מִנְּעֻרֵינוּ וְעַד-עַתָּה] — both we and our fathers [גַּם-אֲנַחְנוּ גַּם-אֲבוֹתֵינוּ] — in order that [בַּעֲבוּר] you may dwell in the land of Goshen [תֵּשְׁבוּ בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן] — for every shepherd is an abomination to Egypt [כִּי-תוֹעֲבַת מִצְרַיִם כָּל-רֹעֵה צֹאן].

---

## Philological Notes

**1. hinneni (46:2) — "Here I am" [הִנֵּנִי]**
Jacob's double-name address and his response hinneni ("Here I am") forge a deliberate literary connection with Abraham's hinneni in Gen 22:1 (the Aqedah) and Joseph's hinneni in Gen 37:13 (when sent to his brothers). The threefold pattern marks decisive moments of divine summons across the patriarchal narratives. The doubled name — "Jacob! Jacob!" — mirrors "Abraham! Abraham!" (22:11) and "Moses! Moses!" (Exod 3:4), the vocative repetition marking extreme urgency and intimacy.

**2. El ha-Elohei avikha (46:3) — "I am God, the God of your father" [אָנֹכִי הָאֵל אֱלֹהֵי אָבִיךָ]**
The divine self-identification here is notable. The title El (not El Shaddai, the form used with Abraham and Isaac in covenantal contexts) combined with the relational "the God of your father" creates a compound that grounds the theophany in covenant history while introducing the unadorned El. This is the last divine night-vision granted to a patriarch in Genesis; after Jacob descends to Egypt, no further patriarchal vision occurs. The anokhi ("I") opening echoes the Decalogue's "I am the LORD your God" (Exod 20:2).

**3. anokhi ered immekha (46:4) — "I myself will go down with you" [אָנֹכִי אֵרֵד עִמְּךָ]**
The emphatic pronoun anokhi ("I myself") stresses divine accompaniment. God descends — the verb yarad is the same used for Jacob "going down" (ered, v. 3) — matching the patriarch's movement. This sets up the theological architecture of Exodus: the same God who went down with Jacob must bring Israel up. The divine descent with Jacob inverts the expected transcendence and foreshadows the incarnational logic of the Exodus narrative.

**4. gam aloh a'alkha (46:4) — "and I will surely bring you up" [וְאָנֹכִי אַעַלְךָ גַם-עָלֹה]**
The infinitive absolute construction aloh a'alkha (literally, "bringing up I will bring up") with gam ("also/surely") adds emphatic certainty to the promise. The verb root alah (to go up) contrasts with yarad (to go down) and anticipates the Exodus ascent. The word order places the infinitive before the finite verb, the standard emphatic construction in Biblical Hebrew.

**5. Yosef yashit yado al-einekha (46:4) — "Joseph will put his hand on your eyes" [וְיוֹסֵף יָשִׁית יָדוֹ עַל-עֵינֶיךָ]**
The closing of the eyes at death was performed by the nearest son (cf. Gen 50:1, where Joseph indeed weeps over Jacob's face). This intimate act — yashit yad al-einayim — is a tender promise that Jacob will die with Joseph present, reversing the years of Jacob's grief over Joseph's supposed death. The phrase is rendered with strict literalism; "put his hand on your eyes" preserves the concrete, physical idiom.

**6. nephesh (46:15, 18, 22, 25, 26, 27) — "soul / person" [נֶפֶשׁ]**
The genealogical formula kol-nephesh ("all the souls") recurs six times in vv. 15–27. Nephesh in these counting formulas denotes the whole living person, not the inner spiritual faculty. The translation renders nephesh as "souls" throughout (matching the established project convention) while acknowledging that the semantic range covers "persons," "lives," "living beings." The LXX renders it as ψυχή (psyche) in all occurrences.

**7. yotse'ei yerekho (46:26) — "those who came out of his loins" [יֹצְאֵי יְרֵכוֹ]**
The euphemism yerekh ("thigh/loin") for male generative origin appears here in the plural construct (lit. "those going out of his thigh"). The phrase is a standard Hebrew idiom for biological descent from a male ancestor (cf. Gen 24:2, 47:29 where the euphemism appears in oath-taking). The translation preserves "loins" as the established rendering for this body-part idiom.

**8. shiv'im (46:27) — "seventy" [שִׁבְעִים]**
The round number seventy (7 × 10) is theologically loaded: seventy nations in Gen 10; seventy elders of Israel (Exod 24:1); seventy palms at Elim (Exod 15:27). Jacob's seventy souls entering Egypt mirrors the seventy nations of the earth, suggesting that Israel's descent constitutes a microcosm of all humanity. The LXX reads seventy-five (adding five descendants of Joseph: Manasseh's two sons, Ephraim's two sons, and one more), harmonizing with Acts 7:14.

**9. lehorot lefanav (46:28) — "to direct before him" [לְהוֹרֹת לְפָנָיו]**
The verb yarah in the Hiphil (lehorot, "to direct, point out the way") is the root from which Torah (teaching, instruction) derives. Judah is sent to "instruct the way" — a role that foreshadows Judah's leadership throughout the Joseph narrative and anticipates the tribe of Judah's eventual primacy. The phrase is rendered literally; "to direct before him" captures the spatial and directive sense.

**10. to'avat Mitsrayim (46:34) — "an abomination to Egypt" [תּוֹעֲבַת מִצְרַיִם]**
The noun to'evah ("abomination, detestable thing") is a strong term used elsewhere for cultic/moral violations (Lev 18:22; Deut 7:25). Here it describes Egyptian cultural disgust toward shepherds — possibly reflecting the Hyksos period's pastoral associations, or priestly Egyptian purity concerns about animal-handlers. The translation renders to'evah as "abomination" consistently with established project convention (cf. Gen 43:32 "for the Egyptians were not able to eat bread with the Hebrews, for it is an abomination to Egypt"). The verse functions narratively to explain why Joseph's family will be settled in Goshen (geographically separate from Egyptian population centers).

**11. vayibk al-tsavvarav od (46:29) — "wept upon his neck again and again" [וַיֵּבְךְּ עַל-צַוָּארָיו עוֹד]**
The adverb od ("still, yet, again") modifying the weeping is difficult. BHS has vayebk... od, suggesting protracted or repeated weeping. The Syriac Peshitta reads "for a long time"; LXX has ἔτι ("still"). The translation renders "again and again" to capture the sustained, uncontrolled character of Joseph's emotion, contrasted with the brief, final word of Jacob: "Now let me die."

**12. amutah ha-pa'am (46:30) — "Now let me die" [אָמוּתָה הַפָּעַם]**
The cohortative amutah ("let me die") with ha-pa'am ("this time / now") expresses Jacob's willingness — even readiness — for death now that the primary grief of his life (the loss of Joseph) has been resolved. The phrase echoes Simeon's cohortative speech (Gen 42:38) and anticipates Jacob's formal blessing and death in Gen 49–50. The LXX renders ἀποθανοῦμαι ("I shall die") as a future indicative rather than a volitional cohortative, flattening the emotional register.

---

## LXX Comparison Table (MT vs. Codex Vaticanus)

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|-------|---------------------|-----------------|------|
| 46:1 | "Israel... to the God of his father Isaac" | "Israel... to the God of his father Isaac" | Agreement; LXX τῷ θεῷ τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτοῦ Ισαακ |
| 46:2 | "Jacob! Jacob!" [יַעֲקֹב יַעֲקֹב] | "Jacob! Jacob!" (Ιακωβ Ιακωβ) | Agreement; vocative doubling preserved |
| 46:3 | "I am God [אָנֹכִי הָאֵל], the God of your father" | "I am the God of your father" (ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ θεὸς τοῦ πατρός σου) | LXX omits the stand-alone El; conflates title |
| 46:4 | "I myself will go down with you [אָנֹכִי אֵרֵד עִמְּךָ]" | "I will go down with you" (ἐγὼ καταβήσομαι μετά σου) | LXX lacks the double-anokhi emphasis of MT |
| 46:4 | "I will surely bring you up [גַם-עָלֹה אַעַלְךָ]" | "I will bring you up" (ἀναβιβάσω σε) | LXX lacks the infinitive-absolute emphatic construction |
| 46:13 | sons of Issachar: Tola, Puvah, Job [יוֹב], Shimron | Tola, Phua, Iasub, Semeron | "Job" → Iasub (יָשׁוּב) in LXX; textual variant |
| 46:20 | Joseph's sons: Manasseh and Ephraim (2 souls) | LXX adds: Machir son of Manasseh, Gilead son of Machir; Shuthelah and Taam and Edom sons of Ephraim (5 additional) | LXX expansion accounts for difference between 70 (MT) and 75 (LXX/Acts 7:14) |
| 46:21 | sons of Benjamin: 10 names (Bela, Becher, Ashbel, Gera, Naaman, Ehi, Rosh, Muppim, Huppim, Ard) | LXX: 9 names with different forms; adds Noeman (Naaman) but omits Rosh | LXX list differs significantly; names reflect different textual traditions |
| 46:26 | "sixty-six souls" [שִׁשִּׁים וָשֵׁשׁ] | "sixty-six souls" (ἑξήκοντα ἕξ) | Agreement on the 66 subtotal (excluding Jacob, Joseph, and his two sons) |
| 46:27 | "seventy souls" [שִׁבְעִים] (Joseph's 2 sons counted) | "seventy-five souls" (ἑβδομήκοντα πέντε) | The famous divergence: MT = 70, LXX = 75; Acts 7:14 follows LXX |
| 46:28 | "to direct before him [לְהוֹרֹת לְפָנָיו]" | "to meet him" (ἀπαντῆσαι αὐτῷ) | LXX replaces the Hebrew directional idiom with a simpler meeting verb |
| 46:29 | "fell upon his neck and wept upon his neck again and again" | "fell upon his neck and wept much" (κλαύσας κλαυθμῷ μεγάλῳ) | LXX uses cognate-accusative construction instead of MT's od |
| 46:34 | "for every shepherd is an abomination to Egypt [תּוֹעֲבַת מִצְרַיִם]" | "for the Egyptians abominate every shepherd of sheep" (βδέλυγμα γάρ ἐστιν Αἰγυπτίοις πᾶς ποιμὴν προβάτων) | LXX active construction; MT uses construct noun phrase; both render strong revulsion |

---

## Structural Note

Genesis 46 is architecturally complex, weaving three distinct literary forms into a single chapter.

**Three Interlocking Movements:**

*Movement 1 — Theophany and Promise (vv. 1–4):* The chapter opens with Israel sacrificing at Beersheba, the traditional patriarchal threshold site (Abraham: Gen 21:33; Isaac: Gen 26:23–25). God speaks in "visions of the night" (a form intermediate between the full waking theophany and the dream; cf. Job 4:13; 33:15), and delivers the last divine speech addressed to a patriarch in Genesis. The structure of the speech is chiastic: (a) divine self-identification, (b) command not to fear, (c) promise of nationhood, (d) promise of divine accompaniment downward, (d') promise of divine leading upward, (c') promise of return, (b') comfort of Joseph's presence, (a') closing with the concrete detail of Joseph closing his eyes. The theophany at Beersheba functions as narrative authorization — God himself endorses the descent.

*Movement 2 — Genealogical Register (vv. 8–27):* The insertion of a formal name-list (toledot-adjacent genre) pauses the narrative momentum entirely. This is deliberate. The register functions as a census — a formal enumeration of Israel as a people entering Egypt. Structurally, the list moves through the four mothers in order of Jacob's wives: Leah (33), Zilpah (16), Rachel (14), Bilhah (7), totaling 70. The number 70 is explicitly stated in v. 27 and is itself a climax — Israel enters Egypt as a complete, numerically symbolic community. The internal summary verses (vv. 15, 18, 22, 25, 26, 27) create a recapitulatory rhythm: each matrilineal group is enumerated, subtotaled, and attributed to its mother.

The 66/70 distinction between vv. 26 and 27 resolves arithmetically: 66 biological descendants of Jacob (excluding Joseph's sons born in Egypt), plus Jacob himself, plus Joseph and his two Egyptian-born sons = 70. The MT is consistent; the LXX's 75 reflects a different recensional tradition that added later descendants of Manasseh and Ephraim (preserved in Num 26 genealogies), harmonized in Acts 7:14.

*Movement 3 — Reunion and Instruction (vv. 28–34):* The narrative resumes with concentrated emotional force. Judah (the surrogate-pledge son of Gen 44) is sent ahead, assigning him a guiding role that anticipates his tribal preeminence. Joseph's harnessing of his chariot (v. 29) is the only such detail in the entire narrative — it conveys urgency and honor. The reunion (vv. 29–30) is rendered in deliberate economy: Joseph weeps at length; Jacob speaks a single sentence. Jacob's cohortative "Now let me die" (amutah ha-pa'am) is the emotional axis of the entire Joseph cycle — the resolution of the grief established in Gen 37:35 ("I will go down to Sheol mourning for my son"). That grief is now dissolved.

The chapter closes with practical instruction (vv. 31–34) — the shepherd/abomination motif serves the narrative purpose of explaining Goshen's geographic separation while simultaneously introducing the social tension that will intensify through Exodus.

**Theological Unity:** The chapter holds together divine promise (God goes down), human obedience (Israel goes down), communal identity (the seventy souls), emotional resolution (reunion), and strategic wisdom (Goshen placement) as interlocking responses to the single crisis of famine and separation that has driven the Joseph narrative since Genesis 37.
